title: Comprehensive Review Of Detection Of Fake Job  Listing Using AI
author: Syed Umraz
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Digital hiring platforms have revolutionized how companies recruit and how candidates search for work, yet this  shift has catalyzed a sharp increase in recruitment scams. Cybercriminals frequently deploy fraudulent job  advertisements to harvest sensitive user data, orchestrate financial fraud, and exploit unsuspecting applicants. In  response, recent scholarship has focused heavily on automating the detection of these deceptive postings by  leveraging Artificial Intelligence (AI), Machine Learning (ML), and Natural Language Processing (NLP). This  literature review synthesizes current research evaluating various machine learning and deep learning methodologies  designed to spot fake listings. Specifically, we examine the implementation of foundational algorithms including  Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machines (SVM), Random Forest, and Naïve Bayes alongside more advanced  architectures like XGBoost and Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (Bi-LSTM) networks. Past studies  underscore that meticulous text preprocessing, metadata evaluation, and robust feature extraction (such as TF-IDF)  are pivotal to maximizing classifier performance. Furthermore, the consensus among researchers points to ensemble  methods and deep learning structures as the most accurate tools for capturing subtle, deceptive recruitment  indicators. However, the literature also reveals persistent bottlenecks: severe dataset class imbalances, rapidly  adapting fraudulent strategies, and the vulnerabilities inherent in analyzing text in isolation. Ultimately, this analysis  confirms that AI-driven frameworks offer a scalable, dependable defense against hiring scams, reinforcing the need  for intelligent verification systems to restore institutional trust and user safety in digital employment ecosystems.

Cybercriminals frequently deploy fraudulent job  advertisements to harvest sensitive user data, orchestrate financial fraud, and exploit unsuspecting applicants. This  literature review synthesizes current research evaluating various machine learning and deep learning methodologies  designed to spot fake listings. Specifically, we examine the implementation of foundational algorithms including  Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machines (SVM), Random Forest, and Naïve Bayes alongside more advanced  architectures like XGBoost and Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (Bi-LSTM) networks. Past studies  underscore that meticulous text preprocessing, metadata evaluation, and robust feature extraction (such as TF-IDF)  are pivotal to maximizing classifier performance. Furthermore, the consensus among researchers points to ensemble  methods and deep learning structures as the most accurate tools for capturing subtle, deceptive recruitment  indicators. However, the literature also reveals persistent bottlenecks: severe dataset class imbalances, rapidly  adapting fraudulent strategies, and the vulnerabilities inherent in analyzing text in isolation. Ultimately, this analysis  confirms that AI-driven frameworks offer a scalable, dependable defense against hiring scams, reinforcing the need  for intelligent verification systems to restore institutional trust and user safety in digital employment ecosystems.
